title: So do I / Neither do I â€” agreeing in conversation
description: Use 'so' and 'neither/nor' to agree with positive and negative statements naturally in conversation.

content.title: Agreeing with positive statements â€” So + auxiliary + I
content.body:
When someone says something POSITIVE and you agree â€” you feel the same:

Use: SO + auxiliary verb + subject

Examples:
â€¢ 'I love filter coffee.' â†’ 'So do I!'
â€¢ 'I'm really tired today.' â†’ 'So am I!'
â€¢ 'I was at the match last night.' â†’ 'So was I!'
â€¢ 'I've been to Goa.' â†’ 'So have I!'
â€¢ 'I'll be at the meeting.' â†’ 'So will I!'
â€¢ 'I can speak Telugu.' â†’ 'So can I!'

The auxiliary verb MATCHES the original statement.
content.grammar_note:
SO + auxiliary + I/we/she/he/they
Match the tense and modal: loveâ†’do, amâ†’am, wasâ†’was, canâ†’can

content.title: Agreeing with negative statements â€” Neither/Nor + auxiliary + I
content.body:
When someone says something NEGATIVE and you feel the same:

Use: NEITHER/NOR + auxiliary verb + subject

Examples:
â€¢ 'I don't like spicy food.' â†’ 'Neither do I.' / 'Nor do I.'
â€¢ 'I'm not ready yet.' â†’ 'Neither am I.'
â€¢ 'I haven't been to the North-East.' â†’ 'Neither have I.'
â€¢ 'I can't swim.' â†’ 'Neither can I.'
â€¢ 'I won't be there.' â†’ 'Neither will I.'

NEITHER and NOR mean the same here â€” both are correct.
Alternative: 'Me neither.' (informal, very common in speech)
content.grammar_note:
NEITHER/NOR + auxiliary + I/she/he/they
'Me neither' = informal alternative
Word order: Neither do I (NOT Neither I do)

content.title: Disagreeing â€” expressing a different view
content.body:
When you DON'T agree (you feel differently):

Use: Actually, I do. / Actually, I don't.

Examples:
â€¢ 'I love cricket.' â†’ 'Really? I don't actually. I prefer football.'
â€¢ 'I don't like the heat.' â†’ 'Oh, I do! I love hot weather.'
â€¢ 'I'm not worried about the exam.' â†’ 'Oh, I am! I'm really nervous.'

Soften disagreement with:
â€¢ 'Really? I actually...' 
â€¢ 'That's interesting â€” I feel quite differently.'
â€¢ 'Oh, I'm the opposite â€” I...'

Never just say: 'No, wrong!' â€” always soften it.
content.grammar_note:
Disagreeing: 'Oh, I do/don't actually'
'Really? I'm the opposite â€” I...'
Always sound warm, not confrontational

sentences: I really enjoy long train journeys. â€” So do I! There's something relaxing about them.
I'm a bit nervous about the presentation. â€” So am I. Let's practise together.
I haven't tried Chettinad food yet. â€” Neither have I â€” we should go!
I can't stand traffic in the evening. â€” Neither can I. It's exhausting.
I love early mornings. â€” Really? I don't actually. I'm a night person!

content.question: Priya says: 'I'm really stressed about the deadline.' You feel the same. What do you say?
content.explanation: Positive statement with 'am' â†’ So + AM + I. 'So am I' = I am also stressed.

content.question: Arjun says: 'I don't drink coffee at all.' You also don't drink coffee. What do you say?
content.explanation: Negative statement (don't) â†’ NEITHER + DO + I. Word order: Neither + aux + subject.

content.question: Kavya says: 'I've never been to a cricket match.' Ravi has been to cricket matches. What does he say?
options: Really? I have! It's amazing â€” you must go.
content.correct: Really? I have! It's amazing â€” you must go.
content.explanation: Ravi DISAGREES â€” he HAS been. So he says 'Really? I have!' not 'So/Neither have I'.

content.question: Meera says: 'I can't cook at all!' Sonal also can't cook. What does Sonal say?
options: Neither can I â€” I survive on Swiggy!
content.correct: Neither can I â€” I survive on Swiggy!
content.explanation: Negative (can't) â†’ NEITHER + CAN + I. The added joke makes it sound natural!

content.instruction: Complete the agreement response using So or Neither + the correct auxiliary.

sentences.text: 'I was exhausted after the trip.' â†’ '___ ___ ___.'
'I won't be in the office tomorrow.' â†’ '___ ___ ___.'
'I hate spicy food.' â†’ 'Really? ___ ___! I love it â€” the spicier the better.'
'I'm really good at cooking.' â†’ 'Oh, ___ ___! I can barely boil water.'
'I didn't enjoy the movie.' â†’ 'Really? ___ ___ â€” I thought it was brilliant!'

content.instruction: Arrange into a correct agreement response.
content.answer: So was I â€” what a beautiful day.

content.instruction: Arrange into a correct negative agreement.
content.answer: Neither have I been to Kerala.

content.instruction: Match the statement to the correct agreement response.

pairs.right: So do I â€” no commute!
Neither am I. I need two coffees before I can function.

pairs.left: I've visited all the southern states.
pairs.right: So have I! Except Telangana â€” I still need to go.
Neither can I. I get nightmares!
So will I â€” we should grab lunch at least!

content.title: â˜… FEATURED DIALOGUE â€” Scenario 2: Office colleagues agreeing and disagreeing â€” Bangalore
content.setting: Three colleagues â€” Ravi, Sonal, and Arjun â€” are having chai in the break room and chatting about work and life. This dialogue features So/Neither structures and natural agreement/disagreement.
content.learning_note: Notice how So do I / Neither do I / Me neither / Oh, I do! create natural flow. This dialogue is revisited in Lesson 13 for speaking practice.

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: I've been really struggling with the new project management software. It's so complicated!

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: So have I! Nobody explained it properly, did they?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Really? I actually find it quite intuitive. Maybe I can walk you both through it?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: That would be great. I also haven't taken my annual leave yet this year.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Neither have I! I keep saying I'll plan a trip and then something comes up.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Me neither, honestly. I haven't even looked at my leave balance in months.

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: I really need a holiday. I'm completely burnt out.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: So am I. The last two months have been non-stop.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: I don't feel too bad actually â€” I've been sleeping much better since I stopped working late.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Oh, I can't do that. I'm a night owl â€” I do my best work after 10 PM.

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: So do I! I can't concentrate during the day with all the noise.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Really? I'm the opposite. I can't work at night at all. But we all get results in the end, don't we?

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: True! By the way â€” do you both like the new canteen menu?

dialogue.speaker: Ravi
dialogue.text: I love it! So much better than before.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: So do I â€” but I wish they had more vegetarian options.

dialogue.speaker: Sonal
dialogue.text: Neither do I mind vegetarian â€” I've been trying to eat less meat recently anyway!

comprehension_questions.question: Who finds the new project management software easy to use?
Who works best late at night?
What does Arjun wish the canteen had more of?

discussion_questions: Count how many times 'So + aux + I' is used vs 'Neither + aux + I'.
Find two examples of disagreement. How do the speakers soften their disagreement?
What do all three colleagues have in common? What is different?

sentences.explanation: Word order: SO + auxiliary + subject. 'So do I' â€” not 'So I do'.

sentences.wrong: 'I haven't seen that film.' â†’ 'Neither I have.'
sentences.correct: 'I haven't seen that film.' â†’ 'Neither have I.'
sentences.explanation: Word order: NEITHER + auxiliary + subject. 'Neither have I' â€” not 'Neither I have'.

sentences.wrong: 'I'm not worried.' â†’ 'Neither am not I.'
sentences.explanation: NEITHER already contains the negative meaning â€” don't add 'not' again.

content.scenario: Your friend says: 'I've never tried sushi.' You also haven't tried sushi. What's the most natural response?
options: Neither have I! Should we try it together sometime?
content.correct: Neither have I! Should we try it together sometime?
content.explanation: NEITHER HAVE I is the correct structure. Adding a follow-up suggestion makes it feel like natural conversation.

content.instruction: Play the agreement game â€” respond to each statement below using So/Neither + the correct auxiliary. Then add a natural follow-up comment.
content.prompt:
Respond to these 5 statements with So/Neither + auxiliary. Then add one extra sentence each time:
1. 'I love street food.'
2. 'I'm not a fan of Mondays.'
3. 'I've been learning a new skill recently.'
4. 'I can't imagine living without my phone.'
5. 'I didn't sleep well last night.'
content.example_response: Let me respond to all five! First: 'I love street food' â€” So do I! There's a great pani puri stall near my office that I visit almost every evening. Second: 'I'm not a fan of Mondays' â€” Neither am I! Mondays always feel like such a slow start. I need at least two coffees to get going. Third: 'I've been learning a new skill recently' â€” So have I! I've been taking online classes for data visualisation â€” it's challenging but really interesting. Fourth: 'I can't imagine living without my phone' â€” Neither can I honestly. Everything is on there â€” work, family, navigation, entertainment. Fifth: 'I didn't sleep well last night' â€” Neither did I! I was lying awake thinking about the presentation today. Glad it went well in the end!

content.instruction: Read the Scenario 2 dialogue and answer these questions in your own words.
content.prompt:
Answer based on the office break room dialogue:
1. What do Ravi and Sonal have in common regarding software?
2. Why haven't any of them taken their annual leave?
3. What are Ravi and Sonal's working style â€” and how is Arjun's different?
4. What opinion do the three colleagues share about the new canteen?
content.example_response: In the office dialogue, Ravi and Sonal have something in common â€” they're both struggling with the new project management software. Neither of them found it easy to learn because it wasn't properly explained to them. Arjun, interestingly, is the exception â€” he finds it quite intuitive and even offers to help them. As for annual leave, none of them have taken it yet this year. They all keep planning to go on holiday but something always comes up and they end up postponing it. About working style â€” Ravi and Sonal are both night owls. They do their best work late at night and find it hard to concentrate during the day with office noise. Arjun is the opposite â€” he can't work at night at all and goes to sleep early. Regarding the canteen, all three seem to like the new menu. Ravi loves it and thinks it's much better than before. Arjun agrees but wishes there were more vegetarian options.

content.instruction: Complete this conversation using So, Neither, or a disagreement response.
content.text:
Priya: I've been exercising every day this week.
Ravi: ___ (1) ___ ___! I started a new jogging routine on Monday.
Priya: I also haven't eaten any junk food.
Ravi: ___ (2) ___ ___. Well â€” I did have one samosa on Tuesday. Okay maybe two.
Priya: Ha! I'm really feeling the difference though.
Ravi: ___ (3) ___ ___! More energy, better sleep.
Priya: I can't believe I didn't start this sooner.
Ravi: ___ (4) â€” I always kept saying 'I'll start on Monday'!
Priya: I won't give up this time though.
Ravi: ___ (5) ___ ___! Let's keep each other accountable.

content.title: Lesson 2 complete â€” So do I / Neither do I
key_points: Agree with POSITIVE: SO + auxiliary + I (So do I / So am I / So have I)
Agree with NEGATIVE: NEITHER + auxiliary + I (Neither do I / Neither am I)
Word order: SO/NEITHER + auxiliary + subject (NOT So I do)
Disagree politely: 'Really? I do / I don't actually...'
The auxiliary must MATCH the tense of the original statement
content.next_lesson: Indirect questions â€” Could you tell me whereâ€¦?
